react-hooks.txt::parent::0:
React Hooks: A Comprehensive Guide
===================================

React Hooks were introduced in React 16.8 and allow you to use state and other
React features in function components without writing a class. This guide covers
all built-in hooks in detail.

1. useState
-----------
useState is the most fundamental hook. It adds local state to a function
component and returns a pair: the current state value and a function to update it.

Syntax:
  const [state, setState] = useState(initialValue);

The initialValue can be any JavaScript value: a number, string, boolean, object,
or array. On the initial render React uses this value as the state. On subsequent
renders the hook ignores the initial value and returns the current stored state.

Updating state:
  setState(newValue);           // replace state with a new value
  setState(prev => prev + 1);   // functional update — use when next state depends on previous

Important behaviour:
- Calling setState triggers a re-render of the component.
- Multiple setState calls in the same event handler are batched together in React 18+.
- State updates are asynchronous — reading state immediately after calling setState
  still returns the old value.
- If you call setState with the same value as the current state (using Object.is
  comparison) React bails out and does not re-render.

Example — counter:
  function Counter() {
    const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
    return (
      <button onClick={() => setCount(prev => prev + 1)}>
        Count: {count}
      </button>
    );
  }

Example — object state:
  const [user, setUser] = useState({ name: '', age: 0 });
  // Always spread to avoid losing other fields
  setUser(prev => ({ ...prev, name: 'Alice' }));

Lazy initialisation:
  If computing the initial value is expensive, pass a function instead of a value:
  const [data, setData] = useState(() => computeExpensiveValue());
  React calls the function only on the first render and ignores it on subsequent renders.

); } When to prefer useReducer over useState: - State is an object with multiple fields that update together. - Next state depends on previous state in complex ways. - State transitions can be described as named actions (improves readability). - You want to extract and test state logic independently. 8. useLayoutEffect ------------------ useLayoutEffect has the same signature as useEffect but fires synchronously after all DOM mutations and before the browser paints. Use it when you need to read layout information from the DOM and synchronously re-render. When to use: - Measuring element dimensions (getBoundingClientRect). - Applying scroll position before paint to avoid flicker. - Any time you need the DOM to be in a stable state before the user sees it. Warning: useLayoutEffect blocks the browser from painting. Avoid long-running work here. In server-side rendering, useLayoutEffect is skipped — use useEffect instead. 9. Custom Hooks --------------- Custom hooks are JavaScript functions whose name starts with "use" and that may call other hooks. They let you extract component logic into reusable functions.
